slots.participant_info:
1. Age: 6 years (born March 2018)
2. Medical: Asthma. Takes Melatonin (5mg). No allergies. No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), weighted blankets, deep pressure activities, climbing walls/soft play, dancing/rhythmic activities, Lego/construction sets, puzzles, pattern-based games, iPad/tablet apps, gaming (Minecraft, Roblox), YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, trains.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Wet clothes, dirty hands, being shouted at, long series of verbal instructions he cannot decode.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or changes to routine, waiting or delays, sensory overload (lights, textures), being told “no”/denied access, fatigue or hunger, group settings or crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, preferred toy/object, quiet space or break, supportive adult nearby, drinking water, chewelries, lollipops.
7. Level of Support: Needs adult nearby and occasional guidance; often requires active adult support; may require 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words or scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + visual supports + game-based approaches; benefits from symbols, schedules, first/then boards; needs 1:1 adult support to process instructions.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs support on uneven surfaces or stairs.
11. Personal Care: Needs physical assistance or supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Needs visual/verbal prompts; requires 1:1 support for most activities; easily distracted and requires frequent redirection.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions; struggles with unexpected changes or waiting.
14. Safety: Very poor sense of danger — constant 1:1 required. Can read safety signs (e.g. “Do not run”) but does not follow them.
15. Other Notes: His poor danger awareness is not easily recognised by others until too late — staff must be vigilant.

1. Age: 10 years (born 10 November 2015)
2. Medical: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD, diagnosed July 2019). No regular medication. No allergies. History of seizures linked to previous ADHD medication (stopped). MRI clear. No seizures for the past 5 months since stopping medication. ADHD assessment in progress (Child Psychologist). No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Lego and building; animals, insects and sea animals; videos; trampolining; hands-on construction play.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Sensitive to other children screaming and loud noise in group settings.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; being told “no”; fatigue/hunger; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks/space to jump; deep pressure; preferred toy/object; visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; breathing/calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support, continuous supervision, and may require two adults to safely regulate.
8. Communication: Uses gestures; Makaton signs; AAC; PECS/visuals to express needs.
9. Preferred Communication: Strong comprehension; benefits from PECS and Makaton alongside clear verbal instructions and gestures.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Reported as largely independent but may need supervision or occasional physical support.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; engagement improves with motivating, hands-on tasks.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in busy environments and around water.
15. EHCP / Professional summary (school educational psychology consultation, Dec 2024): Review to support emotional regulation and managing change. Core profile: rigid/ritualistic behaviours at transitions; disrupted routines can lead to distress and self-injury. Relies on total communication (gesture, symbols, Makaton); sensory sensitivities noted. Behaviour linked to need for routine, predictability and control; structured routines and visual supports are helping. Agreed focus: consistent communication across settings; visual and sensory transition planning; co-regulation strategies for staff and family. Ongoing review with school EP team.

1. Age: 11 years (born March 2013)
2. Medical: ADHD, ASD, Learning Difficulties. Takes Melatonin (4mg) and Elvanse/Lisdexamfetamine (20mg). No allergies. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), weighted blankets, deep pressure activities, climbing walls/soft play, drawing, painting, crafts, Lego/construction sets, puzzles, pattern-based games, iPad/tablet apps (matching, sequencing), Minecraft, Roblox, YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, books, football (watching and playing), card games.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Haircuts and grooming activities.
5. Known Triggers: Waiting or delays, sensory overload (lights, textures), being told “no”.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, deep pressure, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), quiet space or break, supportive adult nearby, calming/breathing prompts.
7. Level of Support: Can sometimes self-regulate with minimal prompts; often needs adult nearby with guidance.
8. Communication: Verbal – full sentences.
9. Preferred Communication: Simple, clear instructions supported with visual prompts.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Independent with toileting/dressing; benefits from reminders and prompts.
12. Task Engagement: Can follow a simple routine independently; may need visual/verbal prompts to stay on task.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Adapts to changes with preparation; benefits from countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: Understands basic safety rules (e.g. pool safety, not running off); may act impulsively – needs supervision in busy or unfamiliar environments.
15. Other Notes: Eiji can be stubborn and resistant to changing his mind; clear routines and gentle encouragement help. Independent in many areas but still benefits from close supervision to remain safe.

1. Age: 8 years (born 20 June 2017)

1. Age: 4 years (born December 2019)

6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, deep pressure, preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), quiet space or break, supportive adult nearby.

1. Age: 9 years (born July 2015)
2. Medical: Severe nut allergy (cashews, pistachios) — carries Epipen. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), drawing, painting, crafts, Lego/construction sets, strong interest in fire exits, pipes, drains.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Screen time (iPad, videos, photos) can trigger stress and obsessive behaviours — currently limited.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or changes to routine, waiting or delays, sensory overload (lights, textures), being told “no”, fatigue or hunger, physical proximity or touch, group settings or crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, deep pressure, preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), supportive adult nearby, breathing exercises and calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words or scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Calm, slow talking; praise and encouragement; writing plans down; using “now and next” approaches.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Needs reminders or verbal prompts.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger — constant 1:1 required. Risk of running off towards objects of interest (e.g. fire exit signs, pipes) and does not stop when prompted.
15. Other Notes: Limit screen use to avoid obsessive or anger-driven behaviours; screens can be used as short reinforcers if needed.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 10 years (born 10 November 2015)
2. Medical: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD, diagnosed July 2019). No regular medication. No allergies. History of seizures linked to previous ADHD medication (stopped). MRI clear. No seizures for the past 5 months since stopping medication. ADHD assessment in progress (Child Psychologist). No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Lego and building; animals, insects and sea animals; videos; trampolining; hands-on construction play.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Sensitive to other children screaming and loud noise in group settings.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; being told “no”; fatigue/hunger; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks/space to jump; deep pressure; preferred toy/object; visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; breathing/calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support, continuous supervision, and may require two adults to safely regulate.
8. Communication: Uses gestures; Makaton signs; AAC; PECS/visuals to express needs.
9. Preferred Communication: Strong comprehension; benefits from PECS and Makaton alongside clear verbal instructions and gestures.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Reported as largely independent but may need supervision or occasional physical support.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; engagement improves with motivating, hands-on tasks.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in busy environments and around water.
15. EHCP / Professional summary (school educational psychology consultation, Dec 2024): Review to support emotional regulation and managing change. Core profile: rigid/ritualistic behaviours at transitions; disrupted routines can lead to distress and self-injury. Relies on total communication (gesture, symbols, Makaton); sensory sensitivities noted. Behaviour linked to need for routine, predictability and control; structured routines and visual supports are helping. Agreed focus: consistent communication across settings; visual and sensory transition planning; co-regulation strategies for staff and family. Ongoing review with school EP team.

11. Personal Care: —
12. Task Engagement: Can follow a simple routine independently.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Can adapt to changes but struggles with unexpected changes or waiting.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger — requires constant 1:1 supervision.

1. Age: 11 years (born March 2013)

1. Age: 14 years (born February 2010)
2. Medical: Epilepsy controlled with Levetiracetam (10ml twice daily). Allergies to eggs, nuts, fish & shellfish, lentils. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), iPad/tablet apps, YouTube videos, singing, riding his scooter.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Particular about textures — dislikes arts and crafts, slime, sand play.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, changes to routine, waiting/delays, being told “no”/denied access, being told to “calm down” (this phrase escalates distress).
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual support (first/then, countdowns), quiet space or break, supportive adult nearby, breathing/calming prompts, repeating schedule regularly, using “now/next” structure.
7. Level of Support: Needs active adult support throughout episodes.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words/scripts; gestural or non-verbal cues.
9. Preferred Communication: Always say his name to gain attention; short, simple sentences (e.g., “first listen to Raul, then play”); visual schedule support; model actions (“Kayden’s turn”, “Kayden try”). Note: he sometimes uses “toilet” or “where’s daddy” as avoidance strategies. Dad’s visible presence helps with regulation.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions.
11. Personal Care: Needs reminders, prompts, and sometimes physical assistance.
12. Task Engagement: Needs visual/verbal prompts to stay on task; requires 1:1 support for most activities.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns; struggles with unexpected changes or waiting.
14. Safety: Understands basic safety rules (pool safety, not running off).
15. Other Notes: Loves swimming and usually engages positively. Parents request notice of instructor changes so Kayden can be prepared in advance.

2. Medical: History of epilepsy (currently in recovery, no medication). No allergies. No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), dancing/rhythmic activities, drawing, painting, crafts, puzzles, pattern-based games, iPad/tablet apps, YouTube videos, movies.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Small children and babies crying; people entering his personal space.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or changes to routine, waiting or delays, sensory overload (lights, textures), being told “no”/denied access, group settings or crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), quiet space or break, supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Uses PECS/visuals; uses gestures and non-verbal cues (pointing, leading adult).
9. Preferred Communication: Short, clear sentences and keywords (e.g. “sit down,” “pick it up,” “turn the light off,” “close the door”). Supported with gestures and visual prompts.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Needs physical assistance or supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Requires 1:1 support to complete most activities; easily distracted and needs frequent redirection.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions; struggles with unexpected changes or waiting.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger — requires constant 1:1 supervision.
15. Other Notes: Usually a happy and calm boy but prefers personal space.

1. Age: 9 years (born November 2014)
2. Medical: ASD, ADHD, Learning Difficulties, Asthma. Takes Melatonin (2mg), Medikinet XL (10mg), Salbutamol inhaler. No allergies, but avoids nuts and egg. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), weighted blankets, deep pressure activities, climbing walls/soft play, dancing/rhythmic activities, drawing, painting, crafts, Lego/construction sets, puzzles, pattern-based games, iPad/tablet apps (matching, sequencing), Minecraft, Roblox, YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, books, bikes, scooting, running, jumping.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Sudden changes to routine or transitions without preparation, being told “no” or having to stop a preferred activity, overstimulating environments.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or changes to routine, waiting or delays, sensory overload (lights, textures), being denied access, fatigue or hunger, group settings or crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, deep pressure, preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), quiet space or break, supportive adult nearby, calming/breathing prompts.
7. Level of Support: Needs active adult support throughout episodes; requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words/sentences; uses PECS/visuals; can express needs and feelings but may go quiet when anxious.
9. Preferred Communication: Calm, simple language; clear step-by-step instructions; verbal + visual supports; reassurance and encouragement; extra time to process before responding.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs support on uneven surfaces/stairs; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Needs reminders or verbal prompts.
12. Task Engagement: Can follow a simple routine independently; needs visual/verbal prompts; often requires 1:1 support for activities.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation/countdowns for transitions; struggles with unexpected changes or waiting.
14. Safety: May act impulsively; no awareness of danger — requires constant 1:1 supervision.
15. Other Notes: Experiences anxiety in unfamiliar environments or when routines change; benefits from reassurance, preparation, and clear explanations. Responds well to calm encouragement, familiar routines, and visual prompts.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 10 years (born 10 November 2015)
2. Medical: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD, diagnosed July 2019). No regular medication. No allergies. History of seizures linked to previous ADHD medication (stopped). MRI clear. No seizures for the past 5 months since stopping medication. ADHD assessment in progress (Child Psychologist). No Health/Emergency Plan in place.

6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks/space to jump; deep pressure; preferred toy/object; visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; breathing/calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support, continuous supervision, and may require two adults to safely regulate.
8. Communication: Uses gestures; Makaton signs; AAC; PECS/visuals to express needs.
9. Preferred Communication: Strong comprehension; benefits from PECS and Makaton alongside clear verbal instructions and gestures.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.

14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in busy environments and around water.
15. EHCP / Professional summary (school educational psychology consultation, Dec 2024): Review to support emotional regulation and managing change. Core profile: rigid/ritualistic behaviours at transitions; disrupted routines can lead to distress and self-injury. Relies on total communication (gesture, symbols, Makaton); sensory sensitivities noted. Behaviour linked to need for routine, predictability and control; structured routines and visual supports are helping. Agreed focus: consistent communication across settings; visual and sensory transition planning; co-regulation strategies for staff and family. Ongoing review with school EP team.

1. Age: 9 years (born July 2015)
2. Medical: Severe nut allergy (cashews, pistachios) — carries Epipen. Health/Emergency Plan in place.

5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or changes to routine, waiting or delays, sensory overload (lights, textures), being told “no”, fatigue or hunger, physical proximity or touch, group settings or crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, deep pressure, preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), supportive adult nearby, breathing exercises and calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words or scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Calm, slow talking; praise and encouragement; writing plans down; using “now and next” approaches.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Needs reminders or verbal prompts.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger — constant 1:1 required. Risk of running off towards objects of interest (e.g. fire exit signs, pipes) and does not stop when prompted.
15. Other Notes: Limit screen use to avoid obsessive or anger-driven behaviours; screens can be used as short reinforcers if needed.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 12 years (born 13 October 2013)

1. Age: 10 years (born 10 November 2015)
2. Medical: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD, diagnosed July 2019). No regular medication. No allergies. History of seizures linked to previous ADHD medication (stopped). MRI clear. No seizures for the past 5 months since stopping medication. ADHD assessment in progress (Child Psychologist). No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Lego and building; animals, insects and sea animals; videos; trampolining; hands-on construction play.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Sensitive to other children screaming and loud noise in group settings.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; being told “no”; fatigue/hunger; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks/space to jump; deep pressure; preferred toy/object; visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; breathing/calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support, continuous supervision, and may require two adults to safely regulate.
8. Communication: Uses gestures; Makaton signs; AAC; PECS/visuals to express needs.
9. Preferred Communication: Strong comprehension; benefits from PECS and Makaton alongside clear verbal instructions and gestures.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Reported as largely independent but may need supervision or occasional physical support.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; engagement improves with motivating, hands-on tasks.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in busy environments and around water.
15. EHCP / Professional summary (school educational psychology consultation, Dec 2024): Review to support emotional regulation and managing change. Core profile: rigid/ritualistic behaviours at transitions; disrupted routines can lead to distress and self-injury. Relies on total communication (gesture, symbols, Makaton); sensory sensitivities noted. Behaviour linked to need for routine, predictability and control; structured routines and visual supports are helping. Agreed focus: consistent communication across settings; visual and sensory transition planning; co-regulation strategies for staff and family. Ongoing review with school EP team.

3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), drawing, painting, crafts, Lego/construction sets, strong interest in fire exits, pipes, drains.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Screen time (iPad, videos, photos) can trigger stress and obsessive behaviours — currently limited.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or changes to routine, waiting or delays, sensory overload (lights, textures), being told “no”, fatigue or hunger, physical proximity or touch, group settings or crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, deep pressure, preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), supportive adult nearby, breathing exercises and calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words or scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Calm, slow talking; praise and encouragement; writing plans down; using “now and next” approaches.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Needs reminders or verbal prompts.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger — constant 1:1 required. Risk of running off towards objects of interest (e.g. fire exit signs, pipes) and does not stop when prompted.
15. Other Notes: Limit screen use to avoid obsessive or anger-driven behaviours; screens can be used as short reinforcers if needed.

1. Age: 8 years (born 20 June 2017)
2. Medical: Asthma. Uses a brown inhaler twice daily (morning and evening). Hay fever in spring. No food allergies. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swinging, swimming, trampolining, building toys, number and alphabet puzzles, messy play (slime, Playdoh).
4. Dislikes/Avoids: New or disrupted routines and unexpected situations; loud or high pitched noises; crowded spaces and social events; some food textures/tastes; clothing tags; hair brushing and teeth brushing; transitions without warning.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; fatigue/hunger; physical proximity/touch; group/crowded settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks; deep pressure; chewy toys; hugs; preferred toy/object; visual supports (first then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision, and may require two adults to safely regulate.
8. Communication: Gestural/non-verbal cues with very limited verbal language (e.g., food, water, toilet, sleep).
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal cues plus gestures supported with PECS/visuals (first then, now next) for clarity and consistency.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance with transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Needs physical assistance or supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; engagement improves with clear structure and motivators.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes and waiting; requires preparation and warnings before moving on.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in open spaces and around water.
15. Other Notes: Frustration increases when needs cannot be expressed; consistent use of PECS alongside calm verbal cues helps reduce escalation.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)

9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 10 years (born 10 November 2015)
2. Medical: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD, diagnosed July 2019). No regular medication. No allergies. History of seizures linked to previous ADHD medication (stopped). MRI clear. No seizures for the past 5 months since stopping medication. ADHD assessment in progress (Child Psychologist). No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Lego and building; animals, insects and sea animals; videos; trampolining; hands-on construction play.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Sensitive to other children screaming and loud noise in group settings.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; being told “no”; fatigue/hunger; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks/space to jump; deep pressure; preferred toy/object; visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; breathing/calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support, continuous supervision, and may require two adults to safely regulate.
8. Communication: Uses gestures; Makaton signs; AAC; PECS/visuals to express needs.
9. Preferred Communication: Strong comprehension; benefits from PECS and Makaton alongside clear verbal instructions and gestures.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Reported as largely independent but may need supervision or occasional physical support.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; engagement improves with motivating, hands-on tasks.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in busy environments and around water.
15. EHCP / Professional summary (school educational psychology consultation, Dec 2024): Review to support emotional regulation and managing change. Core profile: rigid/ritualistic behaviours at transitions; disrupted routines can lead to distress and self-injury. Relies on total communication (gesture, symbols, Makaton); sensory sensitivities noted. Behaviour linked to need for routine, predictability and control; structured routines and visual supports are helping. Agreed focus: consistent communication across settings; visual and sensory transition planning; co-regulation strategies for staff and family. Ongoing review with school EP team.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)

4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.

6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.

14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.

1. Age: 10 years (born 10 November 2015)
2. Medical: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD, diagnosed July 2019). No regular medication. No allergies. History of seizures linked to previous ADHD medication (stopped). MRI clear. No seizures for the past 5 months since stopping medication. ADHD assessment in progress (Child Psychologist). No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Lego and building; animals, insects and sea animals; videos; trampolining; hands-on construction play.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Sensitive to other children screaming and loud noise in group settings.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; being told “no”; fatigue/hunger; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks/space to jump; deep pressure; preferred toy/object; visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; breathing/calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support, continuous supervision, and may require two adults to safely regulate.
8. Communication: Uses gestures; Makaton signs; AAC; PECS/visuals to express needs.
9. Preferred Communication: Strong comprehension; benefits from PECS and Makaton alongside clear verbal instructions and gestures.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Reported as largely independent but may need supervision or occasional physical support.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; engagement improves with motivating, hands-on tasks.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in busy environments and around water.
15. EHCP / Professional summary (school educational psychology consultation, Dec 2024): Review to support emotional regulation and managing change. Core profile: rigid/ritualistic behaviours at transitions; disrupted routines can lead to distress and self-injury. Relies on total communication (gesture, symbols, Makaton); sensory sensitivities noted. Behaviour linked to need for routine, predictability and control; structured routines and visual supports are helping. Agreed focus: consistent communication across settings; visual and sensory transition planning; co-regulation strategies for staff and family. Ongoing review with school EP team.

1. Age: 14 years (born February 2010)

Note: he sometimes uses “toilet” or “where’s daddy” as avoidance strategies. Dad’s visible presence helps with regulation.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 20 years (born July 2004)
2. Medical: No chronic conditions. Medication: Aripiprazole 12.5 mg once daily; Melatonin as needed before bed. No allergies. No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), dancing, YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, shopping, walking outdoors.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Climbing, sitting for long periods, iPad/tablet apps, deep-pressure activities.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or routine changes, waiting/delays, fatigue/hunger, group settings/crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Needs adult nearby and regular guidance; requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Gestural and non-verbal communication (pointing, leading adults); mainly expresses needs through behaviour.
9. Preferred Communication: ABA-based structured approach; 1:1 adult support to process instructions.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Requires physical assistance or close supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support for most activities; benefits from clear visual structure.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger; requires constant 1:1 supervision.
15. Other Notes: May exhibit self-harm behaviours during meltdowns; calm adult support and close supervision essential at all times.

13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation/countdowns for transitions; struggles with unexpected changes or waiting.
14. Safety: May act impulsively; no awareness of danger — requires constant 1:1 supervision.

3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), dancing, YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, shopping, walking outdoors.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Climbing, sitting for long periods, iPad/tablet apps, deep-pressure activities.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or routine changes, waiting/delays, fatigue/hunger, group settings/crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Needs adult nearby and regular guidance; requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Gestural and non-verbal communication (pointing, leading adults); mainly expresses needs through behaviour.
9. Preferred Communication: ABA-based structured approach; 1:1 adult support to process instructions.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Requires physical assistance or close supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support for most activities; benefits from clear visual structure.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger; requires constant 1:1 supervision.
15. Other Notes: May exhibit self-harm behaviours during meltdowns; calm adult support and close supervision essential at all times.

1. Age: 10 years (born April 2014)
2. Medical: No diagnosed conditions. No allergies. No regular medication. No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), weighted blankets, deep pressure activities, climbing walls/soft play, dancing/rhythmic activities, Lego/construction sets, puzzles, pattern-based games, iPad/tablet apps, gaming (Minecraft, Roblox), YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, cars.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Being stopped from preferred activities.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or changes to routine, waiting or delays, being told “no” / denied access.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump, deep pressure, preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), quiet space or break, breathing exercises and calming prompts.
7. Level of Support: Can self-regulate with minimal prompts; may need adult nearby and occasional guidance.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words or scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures, visuals (symbols, schedules, first-then boards), “first/then” sentences.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Fully independent (toileting, dressing); may need reminders or verbal prompts.
12. Task Engagement: Can follow a simple routine independently; may need 1:1 support for activities; easily distracted and requires frequent redirection.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions; struggles with unexpected changes or waiting.
14. Safety: Understands basic safety rules (e.g. pool safety, not running off); may act impulsively – requires supervision.
15. Other Notes: Impulsive behaviour observed.

1. Age: 10 years (born January 2014)
2. Medical: Epilepsy, well controlled with Levetiracetam (2 ml twice daily). Sensitivity (not allergy) to gluten and dairy. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), puzzles, pattern-based games, iPad/tablet apps (matching, sequencing), books, transport.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud noise.
5. Known Triggers: Loud or unexpected noise, transitions or changes to routine, waiting or delays, being told “no”/denied access.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual support (first/then, countdowns), quiet space or break, supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Needs active adult support throughout episodes.
8. Communication: Verbal – uses full sentences but may also rely on limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Simple words, repetition, supported by visual aids (symbols, first/then boards).
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Needs reminders or verbal prompts; may require physical assistance or supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Can follow a simple routine independently; needs visual/verbal prompts to stay on task.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: May act impulsively — requires supervision.
15. Other Notes: —

1. Age: 6 years (born 13 December 2019)
2. Medical: AUHD (family report). No regular medication. No known allergies. No known medical conditions. No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Anything he finds fun; cars, aeroplanes, motorbikes, Lego and creative building.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Very loud noises; struggles with sensory load and uncertainty (details not fully specified).
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; fatigue/hunger.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks/space to pace; visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; reassurance that he is safe.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support, 1:1 continuous supervision, and may require two adults to safely regulate.
8. Communication: Verbal – uses full sentences and may use scripted language.
9. Preferred Communication: Clear, firm instructions; gain attention before speaking; ask him to repeat back for understanding; visuals to support expectations.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent for toileting/dressing, but may need supervision or occasional physical support.
12. Task Engagement: Can follow simple routines at times; often needs 1:1 support to complete activities and sustain focus.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes and waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required.
15. Other Notes: EHCP in place. Supported by a social worker: Naomi Doyle (Lincolnshire County Council). Guardian emphasises reinforcing safety, being present, and calmly but firmly reminding him to listen and learn.

13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and quick, clear transitions.

3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), dancing, YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, shopping, walking outdoors.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Climbing, sitting for long periods, iPad/tablet apps, deep-pressure activities.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or routine changes, waiting/delays, fatigue/hunger, group settings/crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Needs adult nearby and regular guidance; requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Gestural and non-verbal communication (pointing, leading adults); mainly expresses needs through behaviour.
9. Preferred Communication: ABA-based structured approach; 1:1 adult support to process instructions.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Requires physical assistance or close supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support for most activities; benefits from clear visual structure.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger; requires constant 1:1 supervision.
15. Other Notes: May exhibit self-harm behaviours during meltdowns; calm adult support and close supervision essential at all times.

1. Age: 5 years (born December 2019)
2. Medical: No diagnosed medical conditions. No allergies. No regular medication. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Building activities, Playdough, sensory play, Disney Cars.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Changing activities.
5. Known Triggers: Waiting or delays.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump.
7. Level of Support: Can self-regulate with minimal prompts.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words or scripts; uses gestures or non-verbal cues (pointing, taking adult to item); broken speech with gestures helps.
9. Preferred Communication: Short, broken-up speech supported with gestures.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Fully independent (toileting, dressing).
12. Task Engagement: Needs visual or verbal prompts to stay on task.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation or countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: Understands basic safety rules (e.g. pool safety, not running off); may act impulsively – needs supervision.
15. Other Notes: Generally cautious around water, but grows in confidence when supported by an adult.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.

1. Age: 20 years (born July 2004)
2. Medical: No chronic conditions. Medication: Aripiprazole 12.5 mg once daily; Melatonin as needed before bed. No allergies. No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), dancing, YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, shopping, walking outdoors.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Climbing, sitting for long periods, iPad/tablet apps, deep-pressure activities.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or routine changes, waiting/delays, fatigue/hunger, group settings/crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Needs adult nearby and regular guidance; requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Gestural and non-verbal communication (pointing, leading adults); mainly expresses needs through behaviour.
9. Preferred Communication: ABA-based structured approach; 1:1 adult support to process instructions.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Requires physical assistance or close supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support for most activities; benefits from clear visual structure.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger; requires constant 1:1 supervision.
15. Other Notes: May exhibit self-harm behaviours during meltdowns; calm adult support and close supervision essential at all times.

1. Age: 12 years (born 12 May 2014)
2. Medical: Epilepsy (controlled). Epilepsy medication administered morning and night. No allergies. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming and music.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Crowds and anything unplanned or unexpected.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; crowded/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement breaks; deep pressure; quiet break space; supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Requires 1:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, can self-regulate with minimal prompts.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words or scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal instructions only; he understands spoken communication well.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Fully independent (toileting, dressing).
12. Task Engagement: Can follow a simple routine independently when regulated.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from advance preparation and predictable sequencing.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially around water and in the community.
15. Other Notes: Known to Queensmill; family plans for him to attend with his Queensmill teacher.

1. Age: 5 years (born December 2019)
2. Medical: No diagnosed medical conditions. No allergies. No regular medication. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Building activities, Playdough, sensory play, Disney Cars.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Changing activities.
5. Known Triggers: Waiting or delays.
6. Regulation Strategies: Movement/space to pace or jump.
7. Level of Support: Can self-regulate with minimal prompts.
8. Communication: Verbal – limited words or scripts; uses gestures or non-verbal cues (pointing, taking adult to item); broken speech with gestures helps.
9. Preferred Communication: Short, broken-up speech supported with gestures.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Fully independent (toileting, dressing).
12. Task Engagement: Needs visual or verbal prompts to stay on task.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation or countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: Understands basic safety rules (e.g. pool safety, not running off); may act impulsively – needs supervision.
15. Other Notes: Generally cautious around water, but grows in confidence when supported by an adult.

10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Fully independent (toileting, dressing).
12. Task Engagement: Needs visual or verbal prompts to stay on task.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation or countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: Understands basic safety rules (e.g. pool safety, not running off); may act impulsively – needs supervision.
15. Other Notes: Generally cautious around water, but grows in confidence when supported by an adult.

1. Age: 20 years (born July 2004)
2. Medical: No chronic conditions. Medication: Aripiprazole 12.5 mg once daily; Melatonin as needed before bed. No allergies. No Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Trampolining, swinging, rocking, Playdoh, slime, kinetic sand, water play (swimming, pouring, splashing), dancing, YouTube videos, music, lights, bubbles, shopping, walking outdoors.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Climbing, sitting for long periods, iPad/tablet apps, deep-pressure activities.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise, transitions or routine changes, waiting/delays, fatigue/hunger, group settings/crowds.
6. Regulation Strategies: Preferred toy/object, visual supports (first/then, countdowns), supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Needs adult nearby and regular guidance; requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Gestural and non-verbal communication (pointing, leading adults); mainly expresses needs through behaviour.
9. Preferred Communication: ABA-based structured approach; 1:1 adult support to process instructions.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Requires physical assistance or close supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support for most activities; benefits from clear visual structure.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Needs preparation and countdowns for transitions.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger; requires constant 1:1 supervision.
15. Other Notes: May exhibit self-harm behaviours during meltdowns; calm adult support and close supervision essential at all times.

1. Age: 6 years (born 18 August 2019)
2. Medical: No diagnosed medical conditions. No regular medication. Diet often lactose and gluten free by family choice. No known allergies. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Water play (swimming, pouring, splashing) with rubber balls and plastic toys; flashing lights; rhyming music; bubbles; travelling in cars, buses and trains.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Funfair rides; very loud music or raving sounds.
5. Known Triggers: Loud or unexpected noise; sensory overload from lights/textures.
6. Regulation Strategies: Access to a preferred toy/object; quiet break space; calm supportive adult nearby.
7. Level of Support: Can self-regulate with minimal prompts at times; requires 1:1 continuous supervision when distressed.
8. Communication: Non-verbal; communicates via gestures or behaviour (e.g., leading an adult to items).
9. Preferred Communication: One-to-one engagement guided by his interests; keep language simple; model actions and allow processing time.
10. Mobility: Walks independently.
11. Personal Care: Needs reminders/prompts and physical assistance or supervision.
12. Task Engagement: Requires 1:1 support for most activities; easily distracted and needs frequent redirection.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from predictable routines.
14. Safety: High risk near water — tendency to drink bath/pool water and to pick up small floating objects to mouth; constant 1:1 supervision required in and around water and in the community.
15. Other Notes: Usually calm and quiet; may scream if pressured for too long; does not reliably follow instructions or respond without tailored one-to-one support.

1. Age: 31 years (born 17 June 1994)
2. Medical: Epilepsy and Perthes disease (left). Regular medication taken at home. Emergency diazepam used if anxious. Dietary preference: halal or fish option. Health/Emergency Plan in place.
3. Likes/Motivators: Swimming, walking, going to the hair salon, karaoke.
4. Dislikes/Avoids: Loud rooms and busy environments with too many people.
5. Known Triggers: Loud/unexpected noise; transitions/changes to routine; waiting/delays; sensory overload; being told “no”; physical proximity/touch; crowds/group settings.
6. Regulation Strategies: Visual supports (first–then, countdowns); quiet break space; supportive adult nearby; space and time alone to calm.
7. Level of Support: Requires 2:1 support when regulated. When dysregulated, needs active adult support and continuous supervision.
8. Communication: Mainly communicates through behaviour; verbal – limited words/scripts.
9. Preferred Communication: Verbal + gestures supported with visuals; demonstrate first (show, then do); keep language simple and consistent.
10. Mobility: Walks independently; needs adult assistance for transitions or moving between spaces.
11. Personal Care: Reported as fully independent but may require physical assistance or supervision at times.
12. Task Engagement: Needs 1:1 support to complete most activities; benefits from structured guidance and demonstration.
13. Transitions/Flexibility: Struggles with unexpected changes or waiting; benefits from preparation and countdowns.
14. Safety: No awareness of danger – constant supervision required, especially in the community and around water.
15. Other Notes: Supported by a social worker (Sarah Kagaba). Space and reduced demands are important when anxiety increases.
